coredata.dc:title: Solid-state fluorescent carbon dots-based composite optical waveguide film enables transparent and high-performance luminescent solar concentrators
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiurong
Li, Wenjing
Wang, Hangxiang
Zhao, Xiujian
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organosilicon matrices
Solid-state fluorescent carbon dots
Covalent link
Photoelectric devices
High-efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and test of the b-CDs-based LSCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic route for the b-CDs powder.
The photos (a) under daylight and (b) under UV lamp (365 nm) of b-CDs powder. (c) and (d) The SEM images of b-CDs powder.
(a) FTIR spectra of the b-CDs powder and KH-792. (b) XRD pattern of the b-CDs powder. (c) Raman spectrum of the b-CDs powder.
(a) TEM image of b-CDs, inset is the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). (b) The particle size distribution of b-CDs.

Characterization of optical properties of b-CDs powder. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra, PL emission spectra and PL excitation spectra of b-CDs powder. (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of b-CDs powder. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of b-CDs powder. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra, PL emission spectra and PL excitation spectra of b-CDs aqueous solution. (e) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of b-CDs aqueous solution. (f) Fluorescence decay curves of b-CDs aqueous solution. Insets are the pictures of b-CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm light.

Characterization of optical properties of CDs-based LSCs devices with different amount of b-CDs powder. (a) The picture of LSCs under daylight. (b) The photo of LSCs under UV light (365 nm). (c) UV–vis absorption spectra. (d) PL emission spectra (excited by 370 nm).

Device performance characterization of CDs-based LSCs devices different amount of b-CDs powder. (a) J-V curves. (b) Relationship between optical efficiency and loading mass. Insets are LSCs with a load of 60 mg under UV light. (c) Relationship between optical efficiency and storage time. (d) Fluorescence decays of LSCs with a load of 60 mg.
Device performance characterization of CDs-based LSC devices with different volume solution. (a) J-V curves. (b) Relationship between optical efficiency and different volume solution.
The comparison of current work results of solid state CDs.

row.entry: Rotary evaporation, dialysis, and freeze drying

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note. AEAPMS: N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane. CA: citric acid. ODA: 1-octadecylamine. ODE: octadecene. APTMS: 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane.

head.ce:title: Solid-state fluorescent carbon dots-based composite optical waveguide film enables transparent and high-performance luminescent solar concentrators

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine; Key Laboratory of Organ Transplantation, Research Center for Diagnosis and Treatment of Hepatobiliary Diseases, Zhejiang Province, Hangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang University School of Medicine; Key Laboratory of Organ Transplantation, Research Center for Diagnosis and Treatment of Hepatobiliary Diseases

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The First Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine; Key Laboratory of Organ Transplantation, Research Center for Diagnosis and Treatment of Hepatobiliary Diseases, Zhejiang Province, Hangzhou, P. R. China.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a new fluorescent nanomaterial, have drawn huge attention for their outstanding properties and have been applied in biological imaging, sensing, catalysis and optoelectronic devices due to the adjustable optical properties, biocompatibility and low cost [1–5]. However, most CDs encounter the problem of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which causes the fluorescence quenching due to resonance energy transfer and π-π direct interaction when the distance between CDs particles is shortened, and this property seriously hinders the application of CDs as solid forms in optoelectronic devices [6–8]. To solve this issue, researchers have designed solid-state fluorescent (SSF) CDs by one-step and two-step methods [9–16]. The two-step methods are to disperse CDs in matrices like inorganic salts, polymer, metal-organic framework (MOF), and cellulose [17–20]. But the fluorescence properties of some CDs in solution and solid-state are inconsistent, and the quantum yield is also greatly affected by the loading amount of CDs, which will affect the design of their solid-state fluorescence properties. Furthermore, this method is cumbersome and complicated, and requires proper selection of the dispersion matrices, which is not conducive to the development of CDs in solid-state applications.

As for one-step methods, the main strategy is to promote the formation of conjugate regions and a network or rigid structure to facilitate the dispersion of CDs [21–23]. In recent years, the common raw materials for preparing SSF CDs by one-step methods are long-chain alkyl materials, polymers, and silane coupling agents. Among them, amino silane coupling agents (such as N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (KH-792) and 3-aminopropltriethoxysilane (APTES)) are often chosen to prepare SSF CDs by the one-step method [24–27]. The organic long chain is connected to the surface of CDs through the reaction of amino and carboxyl groups. At the same time, siloxane hydrolysis and condensation form a network matrix to evenly disperse CDs to realize solid-state luminescence [10,15,28–30]. CDs prepared with silane coupling agents have good dispersion since they are combined with the matrix through covalent bonds or physical blending, and the composites will not lose their transparency and properties. These hybrid materials have both the fluorescence characteristics of CDs and the coupling properties of silane coupling agents. Due to the excellent performance of silane coupling agents, the hybrid material has the advantages of organosilane: good high-temperature resistance, UV radiation resistance, low surface energy, and outstanding film-forming ability. Because of this feature, these organic-inorganic hybrid materials prepared by sol-gel methods are suitable to be combined with substrates such as glass, ceramic, and fiber to construct multifunctional device, such as photoelectric devices [31].

Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs), composed of transparent waveguides and luminescent materials, show their potential in building-integrated photovoltaics due to the characteristics of collecting and converting solar photons [32]. The luminescent materials are usually dispersed in the waveguide matrices or mixed with the film-forming materials to coat the waveguide materials [16,33–37]. Sunlight is absorbed by luminescent species and the emitted light by them is transmitted to the solar photovoltaic (PV) cell coupled to the edges of the waveguide through total internal reflection. LSCs provide an attractive option for the new architectural design for their tunable shape and color [35]. However, there are still several problems, which mainly focus on the effective absorption of sunlight and the reasonable control of optical losses [38–40]. To fabricate high-efficiency LSCs, the luminescent materials are vital, which should have high photoluminescence quantum yield, broad absorption spectra, and small self-absorption [41–43]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, rare-earth ions, and organic dyes, CDs show wider absorption peaks, adjustable fluorescence characteristics, and photobleaching resistance, and their preparation methods are easier and more environment-friendly, which are ideal candidates for the luminescent materials in LSCs [28,41–52]. Besides, CDs are prone to grafting specific functional groups on their surface, which facilitates their compounding with substrates through specific bonds [10,53]. This contributes to the good compatibility of CDs with substrates, which is important for the optical properties of CDs in LSCs [39]. For example, Wang et al. [53] directly synthesized a new solid-state photoluminescent (PL) silicon CDs (Si-CDs) using a one-step method and applied it to LSC devices (25 × 25 × 1 mm), and obtained two different silane fabricated LSC devices with optical conversion efficiency (η
opt ) of 7.58% and 5.38%, respectively. Sun et al. [10] also synthesized novel CDs/organosilicon composites (OSi-CDs) with excellent solid-state emission and photostability for two different types of LSC (25 × 25 × 1 mm) applications. Thanks to the high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) (∼ 49%) and efficient scattering, the η
opt of the two LSCs reached 4.50% and 5.89%, respectively. Recently, Zhan et al. [15] reported a novel CDs/organosilicone composites (SiCDs) with tunable solid-state fluorescence and PLQYs from 21% to 56%. A series of transparent monolayer and tandem LSCs were further designed using polydimethylsiloxane as the waveguide material, and the tandem LSCs based on blue and yellow emitting SiCDs eventually achieved an η
opt of 7.10%. However, while these works achieved fluorescence of solid-state CDs assisted by organosilanes, they all faced a huge dilemma in LSCs device applications, i.e., the transparence of LSCs devices was too poor leading to too much scattering in the final devices. Therefore, we designed the CDs with long-chain silane to be chemically bonded to the silicone film for better compatibility, taking advantage of the hydrolytic polycondensation of APTES to form films with excellent transparency. Meanwhile, the CDs were obtained in solid-state after the reaction and had solid-state fluorescence, which shows the potential in photoluminescence light-emitting diodes.

In this work, we proposed an acid catalysis method for directly obtaining solid-state fluorescent CDs powder. To shorten the reaction time of silane hydrolysis condensation, we added concentrated hydrochloric acid to the precursor solution and after the solvothermal reaction, the blue emission solid-state fluorescent CDs (b-CDs) were obtained. The b-CDs powder has good water solubility, which could be combined with 3-aminopropltriethoxysilane to form optical waveguide film on the glass to construct transparent LSCs (5 × 5 × 0.2 cm3) with a high η
opt of 2.83%. Furthermore, the b-CDs powder has also been successfully used to prepare monochromatic light-emitting diodes (LEDs), indicating that it has the great potential in optoelectronic devices.

Citric acid (CA) (99.5%), N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (KH-792, 95%) and (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (KH-550, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Ethanol (99.7%) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) (36% ∼ 38%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Epoxy resins (E51 + W93) were purchased from Kunshan Jiulimei Electronic Materials Co., Ltd. The 365 nm chips (1 W) were purchased from Guhoon Optoelectronics Co., Ltd. All raw materials were not further purified before use.

192 mg CA was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and 1 mL KH-792 and 1 mL concentrated HCl were added to the solution. The solution was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined reaction kettle and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling at room temperature, the solid precipitate was obtained from the solution and washed with ethanol several times until the supernatant had no fluorescence. The precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 48 h and then ground to powder. The blue fluorescent CDs powder was recorded as b-CDs powder. As for the experiments in the control group, all were performed in the same way as the synthesis process of b-CDs, except for the selective addition of KH-792 and HCl in between. The control experiments without KH-792 addition were noted as CD1, the control experiments without HCl addition were noted as CD2, and the control experiments without both KH-792 and HCl addition were noted as CD0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and test of the b-CDs-based LSCs
ce:section.ce:para: An appropriate amount of b-CDs powder was dissolved in 0.6 mL of deionized water, to which 1.4 mL of KH-550 was added. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. An appropriate volume of the mixed solution was applied dropwise to a 5 × 5 × 0.2 cm3 glass to be spread evenly and then dried at room temperature for 12 h. The variation of the photovoltaic performance of LSCs and the influencing factors were investigated by changing the loading mass of b-CDs powder and the volume of the drop coating solution. As for the test of b-CDs-based LSCs, after the custom-sized Si photovoltaic was attached on one edge of the LSC by glass glue, and the rest of the edges were uncovered. The uncovered part of the photovoltaic was blocked by the black tapes to avoid any incident light. Then the LSC was placed under a conventional AM 1.5G solar simulator with illumination perpendicular to the surface of the LSC. No reflector or back diffuser was put beneath the device.

The monochromatic LEDs were constructed by mixing the appropriate amount of b-CDs powder with epoxy resin (volume ratio of adhesive A to adhesive B: 10:3) and selecting a 365 nm chip (1 W) for packaging.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) characterization was carried out by JEM-2100F TEM (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) diagrams were recorded using Zeiss Ultra Plus. The instrument employed for the surface morphology of b-CDs was a Nanoscope IV scanning probe microscope (atomic force) (AFM) from VEECO, USA, with a lateral resolution of <0.2 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by Nicolet 6700. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained by a D8 DISCOVER (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out using AXIS SUPRA. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained by Lambda 750 S (PerkinElmer, USA) and a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV2600, Shimadzu Corp., Tokyo, Japan) in the range of 250–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed using QM/TM/NIR (PTI, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes and absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were measured by EI FLS1000 Photoluminescence Spectrometer. The photovoltaic properties of the b-CDs-based LSCs were obtained with a solar simulator (IV4112, Newport Corp., Irvine, CA, USA) at an intensity of 100 mW/cm2 (1 sun). The electroluminescence (EL) spectra of LEDs were measured using HAAS-2000 high-precision fast spectral radiometer (Hangzhou EVERFINE Co., Ltd).

The b-CDs powder was obtained by a one-step solvothermal method with citric acid, KH-792, and concentrated HCl in ethanol (Fig. 1 ). KH-792 plays an important role in the preparation of solid-state fluorescent CDs. It provides functional groups to graft silane coupling agents onto the surface of CDs, which can form a network structure through hydrolysis condensation and effectively separate CDs to avoid the occurrence of ACQ. The addition of concentrated HCl accelerated the hydrolysis and condensation of organosilane parts and the formation of solid matrices. To measure the importance of the experimental parameters, several sets of control experiments were implemented, as shown in Fig. S1. A clear difference can be seen between the three control group experiments. As shown in Fig. S1a, it can be found that the addition of KH-792 clearly enhances the fluorescence and, in addition to that, promotes the formation of solid CDs (Fig. S1b), while the other two control experiments without KH-792 addition did not produce solids. More notably, the fluorescence of b-CDs is significantly stronger than that of the other control experiments (Fig. S1c), which indicates that b-CDs are more suitable for the device fabrication of LSCs.

Firstly, the prepared solid product by the solvothermal reaction was washed three to five times with anhydrous ethanol. Secondly, the washed solid product was dried in a constant temperature oven at 80 °C for 48 h. Finally, the dried solid product was ground to a fine powder using a mortar, which is shown in Fig. 2a, which emits strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) (Fig. 2b). In Fig. 2c,d, the particles are spherical, with a size of about 200 nm and uneven size distribution. It may be because the preparation conditions are high temperature and high pressure, which are uncontrollable, thus the growth of particles cannot be well controlled.

To figure out the composition of the b-CDs powder, we characterized them by FTIR and XRD. As shown in Fig. 3a, the peak around 3300 cm−1 of the KH-792 sample corresponds to the stretching vibration of NH. Because the b-CDs were derived from citric acid, the spectrum of b-CDs shows the stretching vibration peak of OH around 3400 cm−1 and the CO stretching vibration around 1710 cm−1. The peaks around 1100 cm−1 are attributed to the SiO stretching vibration and the CH stretching vibration peaks are present from 2900 to 2800 cm−1. The XRD pattern of b-CDs powder shows two peaks at 22° and 25.6° (Fig. 3b), where 22° corresponds to the amorphous silica structure and 25.6° is attributed to the (002) facet of graphite [54,55]. Besides, the Raman spectra (Fig. 3c) also present D and G peaks at approximately 1356 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1, respectively, the former of which may originate from the amorphous defect peaks of sp3 hybridization, while the latter may be derived from graphitic carbon. The XPS survey spectra of the b-CDs powder reveal the chemical composition of the b-CDs powder namely O 16.3%, N 11.0%, C 54.9%, Cl 9.5%, and Si 8.2%, which confirms the framework of the b-CDs powder is mainly constructed by elementary carbon (Fig. S2). In Fig. S2b, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s is fitted into 284.4 eV, 285.2 eV, 286.2 eV, 286.8 eV, and 288.7 eV originating from CSi, C-C/C=C, CN, CO, and CO, respectively. The fitting peaks of Si 2p are at 102.1 eV and 102.8 eV, corresponding to SiC and SiO. Combined with the XPS test results, it is found that the surface of the powder is modified with carboxyl and long-chain organosilane (Fig. 1). The long-chain organosilane was hydrolyzed and condensed to form the silica network structure, which prevents CDs from aggregation to realize solid-state fluorescence [56].

It is obvious from the TEM image ( Fig. 4a) that many nanoparticles are well dispersed and spherical-like. In the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, an obvious lattice fringe is given and the crystal plane spacing of the nanoparticle is measured to be 0.20 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [42,43,45]. In Fig. 4b, the particle size distribution is between 2.0–6.0 nm, and the average size is 3.76 ± 0.03 nm. Additionally, AFM was also used to analyze the morphology of b-CDs, as shown in Fig. S3. As shown in Fig. S3a, there are particles of different sizes, which are consistent with the SEM images. To measure the size of the particles, two lines were selected for Fig. S3a, labeled line-1 (white line) and line-2 (yellow line), respectively. For line-1 the size of the particles is shown to be about 4 nm (Fig. S3b), while line-2 shows the size of the particles to be about 7 nm (Fig. S3c), which is consistent with the TEM image. During the solvothermal process, KH-792 underwent hydrolysis and condensation under acidic catalysis and appropriate temperature to generate organosilicon solid substrates, and citric acid as the main carbon source generated blue fluorescent CDs. Further, the carboxyl group introduced by citric acid reacts with the amino group of KH-792 to covalently link the CDs in the organosilicon matrix. Therefore, the blue fluorescent precipitates are obtained by the one-step solvothermal.

The b-CDs powder features excellent aqueous dispersibility and the solution exhibits strong blue fluorescent emission under 365 nm light (Fig. 5d). The optical properties of the b-CDs powder and they in solution were further studied. In Fig. 5a, the b-CDs powder has strong absorption in the range of 250–700 nm, and the main absorption peak is located around 412 nm attributed to the n-π* transition of CO. The optimal emission fluorescence peak is located at 470 nm under 400 nm excitation. As shown in Fig. 5b, the powder has a certain fluorescence excitation-dependent property, and the corresponding emission wavelength increases from 470 nm to 522 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 365 nm to 460 nm. The redshift of the fluorescence emission wavelength is especially obvious and the PL intensity remarkably decreases when the excitation wavelength increases to 440 nm. After the b-CDs powder was dissolved in water, its optical properties changed, as shown in Fig. 5d. Its absorption peak changed significantly to 366 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of the b-CDs aqueous solution shifts from 400 to 372 nm and its corresponding emission shifts from 470 to 463 nm. The b-CDs prepared in this experiment were compared with the currently published work on solid-state CDs, and the results are shown in Table 1 . Compared with the current Si-CDs prepared using silane coupling agent and citric acid, the method proposed in this work can directly obtain solid-state fluorescent CDs in one step, which is a more simple method. The former method requires subsequent rotary evaporation or freeze-drying to obtain solid fluorescent powders, which is a more cumbersome step. The fluorescence decay fitting curves (Fig. 5c,f) conform to the double exponential decay kinetics. The average lifetime of the b-CDs powder is 11.03 ns and the b-CDs aqueous solution is 14.03 ns. Compared with the powder, the UV–vis absorption peak of its aqueous solution, the optimal excitation, and emission peaks are all blue-shifted, the fluorescence excitation dependence property disappears, and the fluorescence lifetime becomes longer. In Table S1, the proportion of the A2 part fluorescent lifetime of b-CDs powder decreased compared with the solution. It is because the distance between CDs particles becomes longer when CDs are in the solid-state and the degree of aggregated becomes larger, which causes a redshift in the optical spectrum due to resonance energy transfer (RET) [57,58]. Due to the silicone package, there may be different size aggregates and surface states, which makes the b-CDs powder a certain fluorescence excitation-dependent property.

The LSCs were constructed by directly dripping the mixture solution to the glass and the films were obtained by drying and curing at room temperature, which was a sample and convenient method compared to other methods involving initiator and high-temperature curing. The mixture solution was prepared by mixing b-CDs, water, and APTES. Because the surface of b-CDs is grafted with long-chain silane, this makes them better compatible with silicone matrix derived from APTES, which is more beneficial for the device to have better optical properties [62,63]. To investigate the optimal preparation parameters of the LSCs based on b-CDs, we varied the loading amounts of b-CDs powder and the volume of the coating solution.

As shown in Fig. 6a, the loading amounts of b-CDs were 10, 30, 40, 60, 90, and 180 mg to construct the films, which were colorless and transparent with good light transmission. In Fig. S4, the LSCs show a good transmittance (T > 60%) in the range from 440 nm to 800 nm. Fig. 6b shows the pictures of this series of LSCs under a UV lamp (365 nm), and the bright blue light emitted from the edge of the glass. This series of LSCs have a strong absorption peak of around 365 nm. It is obvious that with loading amounts increasing the absorption peak intensity of the corresponding LSCs is enhanced (Fig. 6c). Under the excitation of 370 nm light, the fluorescence emission spectra of these LSCs cover the wavelength range from 400 to 550 nm, and their emission peaks are located around 443 nm. The optimal fluorescence emission peak positions of these LSCs were blue-shifted from 463 nm to 443 nm compared to b-CDs in an aqueous solution (Fig. 6d). The fluorescence peaks of most CDs are altered by the dispersed environment, which involves pH, organic molecules, and so on [64,65]. This is because their fluorescent properties are easily affected by surface states. Fig. S5 shows the results of the fluorescence excitation dependence test for this series of LSCs, and all the LSCs with different loading amounts were fluorescence excitation independent, which indicates that the CDs in the film have uniform surface states and size distribution as in the aqueous solution. The mass of b-CDs did not affect the UV–vis absorption peak position and fluorescence peak position. When the loading amount increased, the fluorescence intensity increased, and then decreased and the strongest fluorescence was at the loading amount of 60 mg.

From the J-V curves (Fig. 7a) and the relationship curves between load concentration and optical efficiency (Fig. 7b), it shows that as the amount of b-CDs increases, the optical efficiency of the LSCs first increases to the highest value of η
opt = 2.35% and then gradually decreases, and their corresponding photovoltaic parameters of LSCs are shown in Table S2. V
oc is the open-circuit voltage, J
sc is the short-circuit current density, Fill Factor is the filling factor, and η
opt is the external optical efficiency, where η
opt is an important parameter to evaluate the performance of LSCs. The geometric factor (G) is defined as the ratio of the top surface area of LSCs to the edge area coupled to the solar cells. The loading of CDs cannot be increased indefinitely, and when it increases to a certain level, the continued increase may cause energy loss due to the shortening of the distance between nanoparticles, fluorescence quenching, and also an increase in waveguide loss due to increased self-absorption [41,66,67]. As shown in Fig. 7c, the LSCs still maintain good external optical efficiency with good performance stability when stored in the daily environment for 25 days. As shown in Fig. S6a, even after three months of daily placement, the LSC maintained about 98% of the luminous intensity, indicating excellent stability. In addition, when the LSC was subjected to different harsh temperatures, it still maintained about 80% of luminescence intensity even at 80 °C (Fig. S6b). These results indicate that the prepared LSC devices possess excellent stability.

The optimal sample LSCs were tested for fluorescence lifetime and the data fitting curves are shown in Fig. 7d. This data was compared with the fitted data for the lifetimes of b-CDs powder and aqueous solution, as shown in Table S1. The average lifetime of the b-CDs aqueous solution is significantly longer than the other two, while the average lifetime of LSCs is slightly longer than that of solid powder. In the aqueous solution, the CDs have better particle dispersion, wider distance between nanoparticles, and lower non-radiative leaps. The organosilicon film matrix obtained by hydrolytic condensation of KH-550 gives a better dispersion environment for CDs, thus the organosilicon film decreases the possibility of RFT between b-CDs.

The loading amount obtained for the optimal performance (60 mg) was selected for the next investigation of the effect of different volumes of coating solution on the performance of LSCs. The concentration of the coating solution was fixed, and the volume of the solution applied dropwise for coating varied from 1.0 to 2.8 mL. In the UV–vis absorption spectra of the LSCs (Fig. S7a), the absorption peaks of the LSCs were all located at 365 nm. Fig. S7b shows the fluorescence excitation spectrum (λem = 445 nm), from which it can be seen that with the increase of coating solution volume, the optimal excitation peak shows a slight red shift from 380 nm to 395 nm, and the fluorescence emission spectrum also shows a slight red shift from 443 nm to 450 nm. The photoelectric test was performed on this group of LSCs, and the changes in their J-V curves and optical efficiency are shown in Fig. 8a,b. With the increase of coating volume, their optical efficiency gradually increased, and their photovoltaic parameters also changed significantly as shown in Table S3, and their external optical efficiency decreased when the solution volume increased to 2.8 mL. The η
opt of the optimal sample (2.4 mL) is 2.83%. Several factors contribute to the reduced efficiency of LSC devices. Firstly, the dose of the solution volume can seriously affect the thickness of the final film after formation, i.e., the higher the dose, the greater the thickness of the film. When the film is too thin, the interaction between electrons and photons is weakened, thus limiting the energy transfer and electron collection, and the efficiency of LSCs devices is relatively low; while when the film is too thick, the photons cannot pass through the film to reach the fluorescent polymer, thus also leading to a decrease in efficiency. Specifically, when the film is too thick, the photon transmission rate is weakened because after passing through the film once, the photons are reflected or absorbed and cannot reach the fluorescent polymer. Furthermore, when the film is too thick, electrons and holes also need to cross a longer distance to reach the cell, so the recombination rate of electrons and holes also increases, which leads to a decrease in efficiency. Therefore, to obtain the best efficiency, the film thickness of LSCs devices needs to be in a suitable range to ensure both the interaction of photons and electrons, as well as the photon transmission rate and electron transfer efficiency.

Currently, CDs usually suffer from ACQ in the solid state, which severely hinders their application in LED devices. In order to achieve the fabrication of effective LED devices, CDs must first overcome the ACQ problem in the transition from liquid to solid state. Compared with liquid luminescence, solid-state luminescence of CDs has more obvious advantages in practical applications. First, solid-state fluorescence is more stable compared to liquid fluorescence and is less susceptible to external factors, such as temperature and humidity. In addition, solid-state fluorescence can also better protect the fluorescent molecules and extend their lifetime, thus improving the service life of the fluorescent system. Second, solid-state fluorescence is easier to handle than liquid fluorescence, and can be prepared and processed by curing into thin films and coating. These processing methods can better control the arrangement and orientation of fluorescent molecules, thus improving the performance and stability of the fluorescent system. Finally, solid-state fluorescent molecules can be used to prepare various devices, such as solid-state fluorescent display devices and fluorescent polymer solar cells. These devices have better stability and performance and can be used in a wider range of applications, such as display, lighting, energy, etc. Therefore, the synthesized b-CDs powder can be used to realize the fabrication of monochromatic LEDs with a 365 nm chip. From its electroluminescence spectrum at 20 mA operating current, its emission peak is located around 530 nm (Fig. S8a). This shows a large redshift compared with its solid powder (470 nm), which may be related to the effect of the encapsulation material on b-CDs powder. The monochromatic LEDs have a broad emission spectrum, covering the 450–750 nm region with CIE coordinates of (0.323, 0.507), which belongs to yellow-green light with a color temperature of 5714 K (Fig. S8b). The emission spectra of this monochromatic LEDs device were tested at different operating currents, and the results are shown in Fig. S8c. It shows that the working current has no significant effect on the luminescence, and its luminescence intensity and peak are unchanged, which shows the good current stability of the LEDs.

In summary, we prepared the solid-state blue fluorescent CDs by the one-step solvothermal method. Citric acid was used as carbon source and hydrolysis and condensation of KH-792 were catalyzed by hydrochloric acid to form organosilicon substrates. Based on SEM, TEM, XRD, FTIR, and XPS analyses, the CDs were dispersed in the organosilicon matrices by the covalent bonds. The b-CDs powder showed the optimal fluorescent emission at 470 nm under 400 nm excitation, which could be successfully used to fabricate the monochromatic LEDs. What's more, the b-CDs powder has good water solubility and excellent compatibility with silicone film. Therefore, the transparent LSCs based on b-CDs powder were constructed and the optimal device with η
opt was about 2.83% obtained by changing the loading amount and the volume of the coating solution. As of current development, the response of silicon-based solar cells is largest in the red/near-infrared spectral region, while typically presenting very low values in the blue spectral region, mainly due to the tendency to cause severe reabsorption in the blue region. Therefore, we immobilized the fluorescence of CDs by silane grafting to induce them to avoid aggregation-induced fluorescence quench in the solid state and to exhibit excellent stability and dispersion. The prepared CDs with bright luminescence and stability overcame the defects in the blue-light region leading to the realization of relatively favorable performance of LSCs. This work provides a convenient preparation method for the solid-state fluorescent CDs which have been successfully applied to LSCs and LEDs.

Jiurong Li: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Wenjing Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hangxiang Wang: Resources. Xiujian Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Xiao Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2023.122571.
